title: Strategy Pattern
description: Swap the algorithm, not the context — define a family of interchangeable algorithms and let the client choose which one to use at runtime

basic.title: Core Intuition
basic.description: Why the Strategy pattern exists and the problem it solves

concepts.title: The Problem: If-Else Hell
concepts.explanation: Imagine a checkout system that starts with credit card payments. Then PayPal is added, then UPI, then crypto. Each time, you crack open the same class and add another if-else branch. The class grows to 500+ lines. A junior dev accidentally breaks credit cards while adding crypto. Testing becomes a nightmare because everything is coupled together. This is the pain that Strategy solves.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: It's like a restaurant that rebuilds its entire kitchen every time it adds a new dish to the menu. Instead of hiring a new chef who knows the recipe (strategy), they tear down walls and rewire the stove. Absurd, right? That's what if-else branching does to your code.
keyPoints: Adding new behavior forces modification of existing code (violates OCP)
One giant class has too many reasons to change (violates SRP)
Testing requires testing ALL branches even when only one changed
Risk of breaking existing behavior when adding new behavior

concepts.title: The Crux: Context Stays, Strategy Swaps
concepts.explanation: The Strategy pattern says: extract each algorithm into its own class, make them all implement the same interface, and let the context class delegate to whichever strategy is plugged in. The context never changes. The strategy is the moving part. Six words capture the entire pattern: 'The Context stays. The Strategy swaps.'
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: A power drill (context) stays the same forever. You never rebuild the drill. The drill bit (strategy) is what you swap — Phillips head for Phillips screws, flat head for flat screws, drill bit for making holes. Same drill, different bit, different result.
keyPoints: Context = the stable class that USES a strategy but doesn't know which one
Strategy Interface = the contract all algorithms must follow
Concrete Strategies = the interchangeable algorithm implementations
Client = the code that decides which strategy to plug into the context

concepts.title: Brain Triggers: Spotting Strategy in Interviews
concepts.explanation: When you hear certain keywords in a problem statement, your brain should instantly light up with 'Strategy pattern!' These triggers include: 'multiple ways to...', 'different algorithms for...', 'switchable at runtime', 'based on type/mode', 'pluggable', and 'policy/rules that vary'. If the behavior changes but the object using it stays the same, think Strategy.
keyPoints: 'multiple ways to...' or 'different algorithms' = Strategy
'switchable/interchangeable at runtime' = Strategy
'based on type/mode/region' = Strategy
'when X happens, do Y' or 'notify/alert' = NOT Strategy (that's Observer)
Giant if-else or switch deciding which behavior to use = refactor to Strategy

cppCode.title: Strategy Pattern - Basic Implementation in C++
cppCode.explanation: C++ uses abstract classes with pure virtual functions as the Strategy interface. Smart pointers (unique_ptr) handle memory automatically via RAII. The 'override' keyword ensures compile-time safety. std::move transfers ownership efficiently without copying.

javaCode.title: Strategy Pattern - Basic Implementation in Java
javaCode.explanation: Java uses interfaces as the Strategy contract. Each concrete strategy implements the interface with its own algorithm. The Context holds a reference to the interface type (not concrete), enabling runtime swapping. The client injects the desired strategy from outside.

comparison.cpp: Abstract class with pure virtual: virtual void pay() = 0;
comparison.java: Interface: public interface PaymentStrategy { void pay(); }
Simple reference — garbage collector handles cleanup

comparison.cpp: make_unique<CreditCardPayment>(args) — heap allocation via smart pointer
comparison.java: new CreditCardPayment(args) — always heap, GC managed

practiceQuestions.title: Shipping Cost Calculator
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a Strategy pattern for shipping cost calculation:
- ShippingStrategy interface with calculateCost(double weight, double distance)
- StandardShipping: $0.5 per kg per km
- ExpressShipping: $1.5 per kg per km + $10 flat fee
- SameDayShipping: $3.0 per kg per km + $25 flat fee
- ShippingContext that uses the strategy
- Demo swapping strategies at runtime
hints: Start with the interface — one method: calculateCost(weight, distance)
Each concrete strategy implements its own pricing formula
The context just delegates: return strategy.calculateCost(weight, distance)

practiceQuestions.title: Text Formatter
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a Strategy pattern for text formatting:
- FormatStrategy interface with format(String text)
- UpperCaseFormat: converts text to UPPERCASE
- LowerCaseFormat: converts text to lowercase
- TitleCaseFormat: capitalizes first letter of each word
- TextEditor context that applies the format
- Demo formatting the same text with different strategies
hints: The interface has one method: format(text) returns formatted text
TitleCase: split by spaces, capitalize first char of each word
The context stores the text and delegates formatting to the strategy

intermediate.title: SOLID Connections & Real-World Usage
intermediate.description: How Strategy connects to SOLID principles, real-world analogies, and LLD interview problems

concepts.title: Strategy and SOLID Principles
concepts.explanation: Strategy is deeply wired into three SOLID principles. OCP (Open/Closed): Add new strategies without modifying the Context — this is the core reason Strategy exists. DIP (Dependency Inversion): The Context depends on the Strategy interface (abstraction), not concrete classes. LSP (Liskov Substitution): Any concrete strategy is substitutable in the Context — swap CreditCard for PayPal and the Context works identically. SRP sneaks in too — each strategy class has a single responsibility.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: A company's tax filing department (Context) hires tax consultants (Strategies) based on country. US tax consultant knows IRS rules, India consultant knows GST rules. The department doesn't restructure when expanding to a new country (OCP). It depends on 'tax consultant' role, not specific people (DIP). Any consultant can be swapped in and the department works the same (LSP).
keyPoints: OCP (PRIMARY): Add new strategies without modifying Context — the core WHY
DIP: Context depends on abstraction (interface), not concrete strategy classes
LSP: Any concrete strategy is substitutable — Context doesn't care which
SRP (bonus): Each strategy has exactly one job

concepts.title: Strategy's Pattern Web
concepts.explanation: Strategy doesn't live alone. Its best friends: Factory (creates the right strategy), Observer (notifies when strategy changes), Decorator (wraps a strategy with extra behavior like logging). Its evil twin: State pattern — they look IDENTICAL in code structure but differ in intent. In Strategy, the CLIENT chooses which algorithm. In State, the OBJECT itself changes its behavior based on internal state transitions. Strategy = YOU choose. State = IT changes itself.
keyPoints: Best Friend: Factory — creates the right strategy based on conditions
Best Friend: Observer — notifies when strategy changes
Best Friend: Decorator — wraps strategy with extra behavior
Evil Twin: State — same structure, different intent (client chooses vs self-transitions)
Evolution: Strategy evolves into Template Method when algorithms share a skeleton

concepts.title: Strategy in LLD Interview Problems
concepts.explanation: Strategy appears in almost every LLD interview problem. In Parking Lot, it handles pricing (hourly vs dynamic vs subscription). In Ride-Sharing, it handles fare calculation (pool vs premium vs regular). In E-Commerce, it handles discounts (percentage vs flat vs BOGO). The pattern is always the same: Context holds Strategy interface, concrete strategies implement different algorithms, client injects the right one.
keyPoints: Parking Lot: PricingStrategy (Hourly, Dynamic, Subscription)
Ride-Sharing: FareStrategy (Pool, Premium, Regular)
E-Commerce: DiscountStrategy (Percentage, Flat, BOGO)
Elevator: SchedulingStrategy (Nearest, ZoneBased, LoadBalanced)
Cache: EvictionStrategy (LRU, LFU, FIFO)

cppCode.title: Strategy with Factory Pattern in C++
cppCode.explanation: This combines Strategy with Factory pattern. The Factory decides which discount strategy to create based on customer type, so the client code stays clean. The ShoppingCart (Context) doesn't know or care which discount algorithm is applied.

javaCode.title: Strategy with Factory Pattern in Java
javaCode.explanation: Strategy combined with Factory pattern. The DiscountFactory encapsulates the decision logic for which strategy to create, keeping client code clean. The ShoppingCart (Context) just delegates to whatever strategy is injected.

comparison.cpp: static unique_ptr<Strategy> create() — returns smart pointer
comparison.java: static Strategy create() — returns interface reference

comparison.cpp: make_unique<NoDiscount>() — safe default via smart pointer
comparison.java: new NoDiscount() — safe default, GC managed

practiceQuestions.title: Ride-Sharing Fare Calculator
practiceQuestions.description:
Create a Strategy pattern for ride-sharing fare calculation:
- FareStrategy interface: calculateFare(double distance, int minutes)
- PoolFare: $0.5/km + $0.1/min (shared ride discount)
- RegularFare: $1.0/km + $0.2/min
- PremiumFare: $2.0/km + $0.5/min + $5 base fee
- SurgeFare: takes a base FareStrategy and multiplies by surge multiplier (Decorator-like)
- RideContext with passenger info
- Use a Factory to select fare based on ride type string
hints: SurgeFare wraps another FareStrategy — it's Strategy + Decorator
Factory takes a string like 'POOL', 'REGULAR', 'PREMIUM' and returns the right strategy
RideContext stores passenger details and delegates fare calculation

advanced.title: Anti-Patterns, Interview Problems & Pattern Combinations
advanced.description: Common traps, advanced implementations, and how Strategy combines with other patterns in real LLD problems

concepts.title: Strategy Anti-Patterns (The Traps)
concepts.explanation: Four common traps when using Strategy: (1) instanceof in Context — if the Context checks which concrete strategy it holds, you've broken the pattern. The Context should be blissfully ignorant. (2) YAGNI — don't use Strategy when you only have one algorithm that won't change. (3) Fat Strategy — a strategy doing validation, logging, and notifications violates SRP. Strategy does ONE thing. (4) Null Strategy — always set a default strategy to avoid NullPointerException.
keyPoints: TRAP: instanceof/getClass in Context = broken pattern. Move logic into strategy itself
TRAP: YAGNI — only use Strategy when behavior ACTUALLY varies or will grow
TRAP: Fat Strategy — keep each strategy focused on ONE algorithm, delegate the rest
FIX: Use Null Object pattern as default strategy to avoid null crashes

concepts.title: Strategy in Complex LLD Systems
concepts.explanation: In real LLD interviews, Strategy rarely appears alone. A Parking Lot uses Strategy for pricing, Factory for vehicle creation, Observer for notifications, and State for spot states. Understanding how patterns combine is what separates junior from senior developers. The key insight: each pattern handles ONE concern. Strategy handles 'which algorithm'. Factory handles 'which object to create'. Observer handles 'who to notify'. They compose naturally because each respects SRP.
concepts.realWorldAnalogy: A hospital doesn't have one person doing everything. The surgeon (Strategy for treatment), the receptionist (Factory for patient records), the alarm system (Observer for emergencies), and the patient's condition chart (State) all work together. Each has ONE job. That's how patterns compose in LLD.
keyPoints: Strategy + Factory: Factory creates the right strategy based on conditions
Strategy + Observer: Notify listeners when strategy changes
Strategy + Decorator: Wrap strategy with cross-cutting concerns (logging, timing)
Strategy + State: State internally, Strategy externally — know the difference
In interviews, identify ALL patterns in the system, not just one

cppCode.title: Advanced: Parking Lot Pricing with Strategy in C++
cppCode.explanation: This shows Strategy in a real LLD Parking Lot problem. The PricingFactory automatically selects the right strategy based on subscriber status and time of day. ParkingTicket (Context) just delegates to whatever pricing strategy was assigned. Adding a new pricing model (like weekend pricing) requires only a new class and a factory update — zero changes to ParkingTicket.

javaCode.title: Advanced: Parking Lot Pricing with Strategy in Java
javaCode.explanation: Real LLD Parking Lot pricing using Strategy + Factory. PricingFactory auto-selects the strategy based on subscriber status and time of day. ParkingTicket (Context) delegates pricing and never changes. Adding weekend pricing or holiday pricing = new class + factory update, zero changes to ParkingTicket.

comparison.cpp: Strategy + Factory + Null Object — same pattern, different memory model
comparison.java: Strategy + Factory + Null Object — cleaner syntax, GC managed
Returns PricingStrategy reference — GC handles lifecycle

comparison.cpp: Strategy assigned in constructor via Factory — RAII
comparison.java: Strategy assigned in constructor via Factory — simple reference

practiceQuestions.title: Cache Eviction Strategy
practiceQuestions.description:
Design a cache system with pluggable eviction strategies:
- EvictionStrategy interface: evict(Cache cache) returns key to evict
- LRUEviction: evict least recently used
- LFUEviction: evict least frequently used
- FIFOEviction: evict first inserted
- Cache class (Context) with get(), put(), and configurable max size
- When cache is full, use the eviction strategy to decide what to remove
- Factory to select strategy based on configuration string
hints: Cache needs to track access time (LRU), frequency (LFU), and insertion order (FIFO)
The eviction strategy receives the cache's metadata and decides which key to evict
Use a map for storage and additional data structures per strategy

keyDifferences.title: Strategy Interface Definition
keyDifferences.cpp: Abstract class with pure virtual functions and virtual destructor
keyDifferences.java: Interface with method signatures (clean, no destructor needed)

keyDifferences.title: Strategy Ownership
keyDifferences.cpp: unique_ptr<Strategy> — context owns strategy, RAII cleanup
keyDifferences.java: Strategy reference — garbage collector handles lifecycle

keyDifferences.title: Runtime Polymorphism
keyDifferences.cpp: Virtual dispatch via vtable — slight runtime overhead
keyDifferences.java: Interface dispatch — JIT optimized, similar overhead

keyDifferences.title: Strategy Swapping
keyDifferences.cpp: std::move transfers ownership, old strategy auto-destroyed
keyDifferences.java: Simple reference reassignment, GC collects old strategy

keyDifferences.title: Lambda as Strategy
keyDifferences.cpp: std::function<void(double)> can hold lambdas as strategies

bestPractices: Keep the Strategy interface focused — one method for one algorithm
Use a Factory to create strategies when the selection logic is complex
Always provide a Null Object default strategy to avoid null crashes
Never use instanceof or type-checking on the strategy inside the Context
Apply YAGNI — only use Strategy when behavior actually varies or is expected to grow
Each concrete strategy should follow SRP — do one thing well
Prefer composition (Strategy) over inheritance when you need interchangeable algorithms
The Context should be blissfully ignorant of which concrete strategy it holds

commonMistakes.mistake: Using instanceof in the Context to check which strategy is set
commonMistakes.correct: Move logic into the strategy itself — the Context just delegates

commonMistakes.mistake: Putting validation, logging, and notifications inside the strategy
commonMistakes.correct: Strategy does ONE thing (the algorithm). Use Decorator for cross-cutting concerns

commonMistakes.mistake: Creating a Strategy pattern when there's only one algorithm that won't change
commonMistakes.correct: Ask the YAGNI question: will this actually vary? If not, just call the method directly

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to set a strategy before calling the Context method (NullPointerException)
commonMistakes.correct: Initialize with a Null Object default: private Strategy s = new NoOpStrategy()
